topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My stomach clenched, a tight, nauseating knot forming beneath my ribs. The headline, the byline—it all pointed to a stranger. I reread the text three times, the words swimming before my eyes, each repetition deepening the unsettling realization. It wasn't mine. My carefully crafted analysis, the hours I’d poured into that piece, had been repurposed, wearing someone else’s name. A cold sweat prickled my hairline. I started pacing the small apartment, the floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. Every shadow seemed to hold the ghost of the deception. I wanted to scream, to demand an explanation, but my throat felt constricted, dry and useless. I just stared at the screen, the color draining from my face until I felt strangely translucent.

[story 2]

I found the notification tucked away in a forgotten folder, and the world tilted. The familiar rhythm of my day dissolved into a jarring silence. A sharp, metallic taste flooded my mouth. I sank onto the edge of my desk, my hands trembling so violently I had to grip the edge of the wood to steady myself. Looking at the published article, the words felt alien, like reading a stranger’s diary. A profound sense of wrongness settled over me, heavy and suffocating. I began meticulously re-reading the attribution, searching for any misplaced signature, any clue that might explain this egregious trespass. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I kept rubbing my temples, a desperate attempt to physically scrub the intrusion from my mind, but the image of the article, now belonging to someone else, refused to leave my vision.

[story 3]

The realization hit me during my morning coffee ritual. The steam rising from the mug seemed to mock the sudden, icy dread that flooded my system. I picked up my phone, the screen illuminating the damning evidence. My fingers felt clumsy, heavy. When I finally processed the implications—that someone else had leveraged my intellectual labor—a deep, hollow ache settled in my chest. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair slightly askew. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city traffic, feeling utterly exposed. My jaw tightened, a hard line forming where softness used to reside. I didn't know what to do next; the air felt too thick to draw in, and every instinct screamed for a confrontation, yet a paralyzing hesitation kept my feet rooted to the spot.
